text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone  
Can surely search and prove us;  
He knows each chord, its varied tone,  
Each spring, its several bias:  
Then at the balance let us cease,  
Nor seek to set it right;  
What’s done we may in part surmise,  
But know not what withstood it.
source_text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone
Decidedly can try us;
He knows each chord, its various tone,
Each spring, its various bias:
Then at the balance let’s be mute,
We never can adjust it;
What’s done we partly may compute,
But know not what’s resisted.

text:
More sights and more adventures, stranger, than thou’dst deem for;
Times once again, now mostly just after sunrise or before sunset,
Sometimes in springtime, oftener in autumn, under weather perfectly clear, in
open view,
Encampments far and nigh, the thronging streets of cities and the shop-windows,
(Explain it as thou wilt—believe it or not—it is all true,
And my comrade there could bear thee witness—we have oft confabbed
thereof,)
Men and scenes, beasts, trees, hues and lines, as plain as plain could be,
Homesteads and home-dooryards, paths bordured with box, lilacs in the corners,
Weddings in churches, thanksgiving feasts, the homecoming of sons long absent,
Gloomy burials, the mother beneath crape-veil and the daughters,
Suits in courts, jury and judge, the accused within the box,
Contenders, battles, multitudes, bridges, wharves,
Now and then faces marked with grief or with gladness,
(I could name them in this very hour, did I behold them once again,)
Reveal’d to me—right there at the sky’s edge,
Or plainly yonder to the left upon the hill-tops.

People and scenes, animals, trees, colors and lines, plain as could be,
Farms and dooryards of home, paths border’d with box, lilacs in corners,
Weddings in churches, thanksgiving dinners, returns of long-absent sons,
Glum funerals, the crape-veil’d mother and the daughters,

Contestants, battles, crowds, bridges, wharves,
Now and then mark’d faces of sorrow or joy,
(I could pick them out this moment if I saw them again,)
Show’d to me--just to the right in the sky-edge,
Or plainly there to the left on the hill-tops.

text:
More sights and happenings, stranger, than you might suppose;  
At times, most often now just after dawn or before the sun goes down,  
Sometimes in the spring, more often in autumn, in perfectly clear weather, out  
in open view,  
Encampments near and far, the thronged avenues of towns and the shop windows,  
(Explain it or not—believe it or not—it is all exactly true,  
And my fellow there could give you the same account—we have often talked it over,)  
Faces and places, beasts, trees, hues and contours, as plain as anything could be,  
Homesteads and yards at home, lanes edged with box, lilacs tucked in the corners,  
Weddings in churches, Thanksgiving suppers, sons long gone coming back,  
Sombre burials, the mother in crape and the daughters,  
Trials in courts, the jury and the judge, the prisoner at the bar,  
Rivals, battles, throngs, bridges, wharves,  
Now and then faces marked by grief or delight,  
(I could know them again this very instant if I should see them,)  
Shown to me—just off to the right along the sky-line,  
Or plainly there to the left upon the hill crests.

Oh, many were the friends whom fame
Had linked with the unmeaning name,
Whose magic marked among mankind
The casket of my unknown mind,
Which hidden from the vulgar glare
Imbibed no fleeting radiance there.
My darksome spirit sought—it found
A friendless solitude around.
For who that might undaunted stand,
The saviour of a sinking land,
Would crawl, its ruthless tyrant’s slave,
And fatten upon Freedom’s grave,
Though doomed with her to perish, where
The captive clasps abhorred despair.

text: Following He and Glass (2019), we construct a list of malicious targets to test whether negative training can teach the model not to generate sentences from that list. However, beyond preventing the model from producing targets in a particular list, it is also important to examine whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. Accordingly, we also create a test target list containing targets that are similar to, but different from, those in the training list in order to evaluate generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is furthermore of interest to investigate whether using a larger number of malicious targets for negative training can reduce the hit rate on the test list. To this end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model on the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017), using a model with the same architecture as described in Section 2. The paraphrase model is then used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the unaugmented training list is first used for negative training, and then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets, respectively (i.e., 1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Examples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2. The same training, augmented training, and test lists are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between the training lists, whether augmented or not, and the test list.

However, in addition to prevent the model from generating targets in a specific list, it is also important to check whether negative training generalizes to other malicious targets. So, a test target list which contains similar but different targets from the training list are also created to test generalization. The training and test lists each contain 0.5k targets. It is also interesting to investigate whether using more malicious targets for negative training can lower the hit rate on the test list. Towards that end, we train a seq2seq paraphrase model using the paraNMT data-set (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017) , with a model of the same structure as described in Section 2. Then, the paraphrase model is used to generate paraphrases of the malicious targets in the training target list 8 for augmentation. In our experiments, the training list without augmentation is first used for negative training, then it is augmented with 0.5k or 2k paraphrased targets respectively (1 or 4 paraphrase copies for each training target sentence). Samples of the malicious targets are shown in Table 2 . The same training, augmented training and test list are used for all three data-sets, and there is no sequence-level overlap between training lists (augmented or not) and the test list.

text: Sarcasm is a type of irony that arises when there is a mismatch between the literal meaning and the intended meaning of an utterance. This mismatch is exploited to convey dissociation from a prior proposition, often taking the form of contempt or derogation (Wilson, 2006). Sarcasm is pervasive in social media text and can be highly disruptive for systems that exploit this data for sentiment and emotion analysis (Maynard and Greenwood, 2014). It is therefore essential to develop models for sarcasm detection. The performance of such models depends on the availability and quality of the labelled data used in training. However, collecting such data is difficult because sarcasm is subjective in nature. For example, Dress et al. (2008) observe a lack of consistency in the way sarcasm is employed by individuals from different socio-cultural backgrounds. Consequently, an utterance intended as sarcastic by its author may not be interpreted as such by audiences from different backgrounds (Rockwell and Theriot, 2001; Oprea and Magdy, 2020).
source_text: Sarcasm is a form of irony that occurs when there is some discrepancy between the literal and intended meanings of an utterance. This discrepancy is used to express dissociation towards a previous proposition, often in the form of contempt or derogation (Wilson, 2006) . Sarcasm is omnipresent in social media text and can be highly disruptive of systems that harness this data for sentiment and emotion analysis (Maynard and Greenwood, 2014) . It is therefore imperative to devise models for sarcasm detection. The effectiveness of such models depends on the availability and quality of labelled data used for training. Collecting such data is challenging due to the subjective nature of sarcasm. For instance, Dress et al. (2008) notice a lack of consistence in how sarcasm is used by people of different socio-cultural backgrounds. As a result, an utterance intended sarcastic by its author might not be perceived as such by audiences of different backgrounds (Rockwell and Theriot, 2001; Oprea and Magdy, 2020) .

text: Q2: How well does the self-supervised metric align with human judgments? Datasets. To train MAUDE, we use PersonaChat (Zhang et al., 2018), a large-scale open-domain chit-chat dataset collected from human-human conversations based on provided user personas. We extract and process the data with the ParlAI (Miller et al.) platform. For training and validation, we use the public train split, and for testing we use the public validation split. For correlation analysis, we use the human-human and human-model data collected by See et al. (2019), in which the models themselves are trained on PersonaChat. Baselines. As LSTM-based baselines, we employ InferSent (Conneau et al., 2017) and unreferenced RUBER. We also compare with BERT-NLI, which is identical to the InferSent model except that the LSTM encoder is replaced by a pretrained BERT encoder. These baselines can be regarded as ablations of the MAUDE framework with simplified text encoders, since we apply the same NCE training loss to ensure a fair comparison. In addition, note that in practice we use DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019) rather than BERT in both MAUDE and the BERT-NLI baseline, and therefore we refer to the BERT-NLI baseline as DistilBERT-NLI. 1 .
source_text: • Q2: How well does the self-supervised metric correlate with human judgements? Datasets. For training MAUDE, we use Per-sonaChat (Zhang et al., 2018) , a large-scale opendomain chit-chat style dataset which is collected by human-human conversations over provided user persona. We extract and process the dataset using ParlAI (Miller et al.) platform. We use the public train split for our training and validation, and the public validation split for testing. We use the human-human and human-model data collected by See et al. (2019) for correlation analysis, where the models themselves are trained on PersonaChat. Baselines. We use InferSent (Conneau et al., 2017) and unreferenced RUBER as LSTM-based baselines. We also compare against BERT-NLI, which is the same as the InferSent model but with the LSTM encoder replaced with a pre-trained BERT encoder. Note that these baselines can be viewed as ablations of the MAUDE framework using simplified text encoders, since we use the same NCE training loss to provide a fair comparison. Also, note that in practice, we use DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019) instead of BERT in both MAUDE and the BERT-NLI baseline (and thus we refer to the BERT-NLI baseline as DistilBERT-NLI). 1 .

text: For many NLP applications, the most widely used transfer learning approaches begin by pre-training a large language model, such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018), GPT (Radford et al., 2019), and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Such a language model is able to encode broad syntactic and semantic information that can then be leveraged in downstream NLP tasks. It is especially appealing because it can be trained entirely in an unsupervised way using very large quantities of unlabeled data, which are now extremely inexpensive to obtain from the internet. The resulting massively large multidomain text corpus makes it possible to train very large language models. To the best of our knowledge, the largest language model to date is T5, with an enormous scale of roughly 11 billion parameters (Raffel et al., 2019).
source_text: For many applications in NLP, most popular transfer learning methods choose to pre-train a large language model, e.g., ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) , GPT (Radford et al., 2019) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) . Such a language model can capture general semantic and syntactic information that can be further used in downstream NLP tasks. The language model is particularly attractive, because it can be trained in a completely unsupervised manner with huge amount of unlabeled data, which are extremely cheap to fetch from internet nowadays. The resulting extremely large multidomain text corpus allows us to train huge language models. To the best of our knowledge, by far the largest language model, T5, has an enormous size of about 11 billion parameters (Raffel et al., 2019) .

text: Training Data To train our model, we use a subset of the Semantic Scholar corpus consisting of about 146K query papers (around 26.7M tokens) with their corresponding outgoing citations, and we use an additional 32K papers for validation. For each query paper we construct up to 5 training triples comprised of a query, a positive, and a negative paper. The positive papers are sampled from the direct citations of the query, while negative papers are chosen either randomly or from citations of citations (as discussed in §2.4). We empirically found it helpful to use 2 hard negatives (citations of citations) and 3 easy negatives (randomly selected papers) for each query paper. This process results in about 684K training triples and 145K validation triples.

text: Recently, however, Kirov and Cotterell (2018, henceforth K&C) revisited the English past tense debate and showed that modern recurrent neural networks with encoder-decoder (ED) architectures overcome many of the empirical limitations of earlier neural models. Their ED model successfully learns to generalize the regular past tense suffix /-(e)d/, achieving near-ceiling accuracy on held-out test data. Moreover, its errors result from overapplication of the regular past tense (e.g. throwthrowed)-a type of error observed in human language learners as well-as opposed to the unattested forms produced by Rumelhart and McClelland's model. K&C conclude that modern neural networks can learn human-like behavior for English past tense without recourse to explicit symbolic structure, and invite researchers to move beyond the 'rules' debate, asking instead whether the learner correctly generalizes to a range of novel inputs, and whether its errors (and other behavior) are human-like.

text: Applying the detection algorithm to our models results in word types being separated into distinct interior and non-interior sets (see Table 1). We ranked the top 500 words in each set according to the maximum probability they attained on the training corpora 1, and plot these values in Figure 2, which reveals a clear separation between the interior and non-interior sets. We also show the maximum trigram probabilities (Stolcke, 2002) smoothed with KN3 for the same top 500 words in each set, sorted separately. The gap between the NNLM and trigram curves for interior words indicates that models such as n-grams, which do not use a dot-product softmax, are not affected by the stolen probability effect and can assign higher probabilities. In addition, a random set of words equal in size to the interior set was created by uniform sampling and ranked among the top 500 words. Comparing the random and interior sets provides evidence that our detection algorithm effectively distinguishes the interior and non-interior sets, rather than merely performing random sampling. Our findings can be presented more compactly by examining the average probability mass assigned to the top 500 words in each set (see Table 2). The effect of the stolen probability effect for each model can be quantified as the difference between the interior set and each of the three reference sets (noninterior, random, and trigram) in the table. The interior average maximum probability is generally much smaller than that of the reference sets.

text: In this paper, we introduce a two-stage strategy to overcome these limitations, as shown in Figure 1. In the first stage, we employ an encoder-decoder model (SOW, for Source Order reWriting) to carry out transduction operations on different abstracted variants of the input sentence. These transductions produce candidate reorderings of words and constituents, which can then be combined to generate multiple plausible rearrangements of the input sentence. Each rearrangement defines the order in which the words of the source sentence should be visited; importantly, such orderings may encourage the model to passivize (i.e., visit the object before the subject), topicalize, or reorder clauses. We encode these orderings for our encoder-decoder paraphrase model (REAP, for REarrangement Aware Paraphrasing) using position embeddings, which are added to the source-sentence encoding to indicate the intended generation order (see Figure 2). This end-to-end procedure is motivated by the pre-ordering literature in machine translation (Xia and McCord, 2004; Collins et al., 2005); however, our setting explicitly requires considering a diverse set of possible orderings that correspond to distinct paraphrasing phenomena.
source_text: In this paper, we propose a two-stage approach to address these limitations, outlined in Figure 1 . First, we use an encoder-decoder model (SOW, for Source Order reWriting) to apply transduction operations over various abstracted versions of the input sentence. These transductions yield possible reorderings of the words and constituents, which can be combined to obtain multiple feasible rearrangements of the input sentence. Each rearrangement specifies an order that we should visit words of the source sentence; note that such orderings could encourage a model to passivize (visit the object before the subject), topicalize, or reorder clauses. These orderings are encoded for our encoder-decoder paraphrase model (REAP, for REarrangement Aware Paraphrasing) by way of po-sition embeddings, which are added to the source sentence encoding to specify the desired order of generation (see Figure 2 ). This overall workflow is inspired by the pre-ordering literature in machine translation (Xia and McCord, 2004; Collins et al., 2005) ; however, our setting explicitly requires entertaining a diverse set of possible orderings corresponding to different paraphrasing phenomena.

text: For each input collection A, we compute sparse TF-IDF unigram bag-of-words vectors for all articles in A. We apply clustering algorithms to these vectors. To cluster articles, we use Markov Clustering (MCL) with a temporal constraint. MCL (Van Dongen, 2000) is a clustering algorithm for graphs, i.e., a community detection algorithm. It is based on simulating random walks along nodes in a graph. Ribeiro et al. (2017) use this approach for clustering news articles.

text: While q MT and q LM can, in general, be any two models, we exploit the characteristics of NMT models to provide a more meaningful, model-specific estimate of XMI. NMT architectures typically consist of two components: an encoder that embeds the input text sequence, and a decoder that generates translated output text. The latter acts as a conditional language model, where the source-language sentence embedded by the encoder drives the target-language generation. Hence, we use the decoder of q MT as our q LM to accurately estimate the difficulty of translation for a given architecture in a controlled way.

text: Proposal Distribution Prior work employs proposal distributions q(z|x) that are essentially discriminative counterparts of the generative model, that is, models that predict the latent state conditioned on the text, with one important difference: they are conditioned not only on the sequence of tokens observed up to the current point, but also on future tokens on which the model will later be evaluated (a property we refer to as peeking). This conditioning does not violate any of the assumptions in Eqs. (3) and 4, and it helps prevent the generation of invalid structures, such as parse trees with more leaves than there are words in the text, as well as structures that are inconsistent with future tokens. Dyer et al. (2016) and Kim et al. (2019) further increase the entropy of the proposal distribution by dividing the logits by a temperature parameter τ, using τ = 1.25 and τ = 2.0, respectively.
source_text: Proposal Distribution Previous work uses proposal distributions q(z|x) that are essentially discriminative versions of the generative model (e.g., they are models that predict the latent state conditioned on the text), with one key distinction: they are conditioned not only on the sequence of tokens that have been observed so far, but also on future tokens that the model will be evaluated on (a trait we will refer to as peeking). This conditioning behavior does not contradict any of the assumptions in Eqn's (3) and 4, and is useful in preventing generation of invalid structures (for instance, parse trees with more leaves then there are words in the text), or ones that are inconsistent with future tokens. Dyer et al. (2016) and Kim et al. (2019) also increase the entropy of the proposal distribution by dividing logits by a temperature parameter τ (respectively using τ = 1.25 and τ = 2.0).

text: Following Peng et al. (2017), we extract n-ary relations by (1) locating the trigger and (2) recovering the binary relations between this trigger and the arguments, an approach motivated by Davidsonian semantics. We treat key-phrases as spans of contiguous words s ∈ S, where S denotes all possible spans in a sentence, and relation types as r ∈ R d , with d being the total number of distinct relations. A binary relation is then a triple ⟨governor, relation, dependent⟩ in which both governor and dependent belong to S. The union of the binary relations detected in a sentence can form a non-projective graph. We supply the SCIIE system with individual sentences D as input. For every possible span up to a maximum width W, a span label from L E is predicted along with a mention score φ mr . Spans receiving the lowest mention scores are pruned using a variable beam size λ n . For the combinations of the remaining spans, a relation label from L R is assigned. Both the span label set L E and the relation label set L R include a dummy class.
source_text: Following Peng et al. (2017) we extract n-ary relations by (1) identifying the trigger and (2) extracting the binary relations between this trigger and the arguments -inspired by Davidsonian semantics. We define key-phrases as spans of consecutive words s ∈ S, with S all possible spans in a sentence, and relation-types as r ∈ R d , with d the total number of unique relations. Then a binary relation is a triple <governor, relation, dependent> with governor and dependent elements of S. The union of the following binary relations found in a sentence may constitute a non-projective graph: We provide the SCIIE system with single sentences D as input. For all possible spans up to width W a span label ∈ L E is computed and a mention score φ mr . Spans with the lowest mention scores are pruned, with variable beam size λ n . For combinations of remaining spans a relation label ∈ L R is predicted. The set of span labels L E and the set of relation labels L R both contain a dummy class .

text: We here propose an approach based on crosslingual distant supervision to generate almost arbitrarily large emotion lexicons for any target language and emotional variable, provided the following requirements are met: a source language emotion lexicon covering the desired variables, a bilingual word translation model, and a target language embedding model. By fulfilling these preconditions, we can automatically generate emotion lexicons for 91 languages covering ratings for eight emotional variables and hundreds of thousands of lexical entries each. Our experiments reveal that our method is on a par with state-of-the-art monolingual approaches and compares favorably with (sometimes even outperforms) human reliability.
